elements.sent-2411-001: Content-based Recommender Systems (CRSs) play a crucial role in shaping user experiences in e-commerce, online advertising, and personalized recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, due to the vast amount of categorical features, the embedding tables used in CRS models pose a significant storage bottleneck for real-world deployment, especially on resource-constrained devices.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this problem, various embedding pruning methods have been proposed, but most existing ones require expensive retraining steps for each target parameter budget, leading to enormous computation costs.
elements.sent-2411-004: In reality, this computation cost is a major hurdle in real-world applications with diverse storage requirements, such as federated learning and streaming settings.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we propose Shapley Value-guided Embedding Reduction (Shaver) as our response.
elements.sent-2411-006: With Shaver, we view the problem from a cooperative game perspective, and quantify each embedding parameter's contribution with Shapley values to facilitate contribution-based parameter pruning.
elements.sent-2411-007: To address the inherently high computation costs of Shapley values, we propose an efficient and unbiased method to estimate Shapley values of a CRS's embedding parameters.
elements.sent-2411-008: Moreover, in the pruning stage, we put forward a field-aware codebook to mitigate the information loss in the traditional zero-out treatment.
elements.sent-2411-009: Through extensive experiments on three real-world datasets, Shaver has demonstrated competitive performance with lightweight recommendation models across various parameter budgets.
elements.sent-2411-010: The source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/shaver-E808.
elements.sent-2411-011: In the modern era, recommender systems (RSs) play a vital role in assisting users to identify relevant information.
elements.sent-2411-012: Meanwhile, on top of user-item interactions, the increasingly available side features of users and items (e.g., user gender and product category) have made content-based RSs (CRSs) \cite{marcuzzo2022recommendation a step above collaborative filtering-based RSs, especially in cold-start settings.}
elements.sent-2411-013: According to the Statista report \cite{statista2024 , in the US, online advertising -- a major CRS application -- has doubled its market size from \$107.5 billion in 2018 to \$225 billion in 2023.}
elements.sent-2411-014: More recently, with privacy legislation and cyber security awareness on the rise, on-device deployment of CRSs is quickly gaining popularity, where examples include Brave Browser's federated news recommendation \cite{minto2021 , as well as Kuaishou's short video recommendation on mobile devices \cite{gong2022real .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Most modern CRSs depend on sparse categorical features, such as movie genres and user regions in movie recommendation \cite{marcuzzo2022recommendation , where a dense embedding table that hosts all possible features' embeddings (i.e., vector representations) is trained.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Given the enormous amount of sparse categorical features, embedding tables have become the storage bottleneck for modern CRSs, which contradicts the surging demand for scalability and efficiency during on-device deployment \cite{yin2024device,memcom2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: For illustration, in click-through rate (CTR) prediction, a typical CRS task that predicts users' clicking intention on an item, the RS deployed by Meta has been reported to consume 12T parameters and can demand up to 96TB of memory and multiple GPUs to train \cite{metarecsys2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Consequently, lowering the parameter footprint has attracted immense attention in both industry applications \cite{memcom2020,nis2019 and academic research \cite{bet2024,han2021deeprec .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: For instance, PEP \cite{pep2021 prunes the embedding table by searching a pruning mask through a learnable threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-020: After the initial training of both the threshold and the model, the model is retrained from scratch to work under the pruned setting.
elements.sent-2411-021: Another example is AutoSrh \cite{kong2022autosrh , which adaptively groups features into blocks.}
elements.sent-2411-022: After bi-level optimization, a sparse embedding parameterization can be selected according to the memory budget and then optimized via retraining.
elements.sent-2411-023: Generally, these methods focus on pruning the embedding table to a fixed size, hence an expensive training cycle is required for every single target sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-024: On one side, in most existing solutions, optimal trade-offs between recommendation accuracy and parameter efficiency require tailoring models according to each device's specific computational and storage capacities.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, on the flip side of the coin, such expensive, repetitive training procedures are not affordable in practical settings with varying on-device storage needs.
elements.sent-2411-027: Scenario 1: Federated recommendation ensures privacy by assigning each user an on-device recommender.
elements.sent-2411-028: Such paradigms commonly suffer from hardware heterogeneity, where the variation of computing resources across devices (e.g., TV boxes, smart watches, and mobile phones) \cite{rule2021,yin2024device prohibits a uniform model architecture from being used.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This naturally calls for more efficient means of embedding pruning to provide multiple versions of a trained model.
elements.sent-2411-030: Scenario 2: When deployed on-device, a model needs to operate under different computational resource budgets due to software parallelization and varying battery status \cite{yu2019slimmable,cai2020once , and CRSs are no exception.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Considering the time-sensitive nature of recommendation services, a sophisticated embedding pruning mechanism that requires reinforcement learning \cite{bet2024 or even post-pruning retraining \cite{pep2021 will lead to a significant overhead, hindering the usability of the service pipeline.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Ideally, considering those real-life scenarios, a competent pruning technique is expected to quickly adapt a well-trained CRS model to any specified parameter budget, without undergoing iterative search or retraining that are both time-consuming.
elements.sent-2411-033: Therefore, this paper focuses on a more practical, yet largely neglected setting of CRS compression for on-device deployment, which is to prune the embedding table of a recommender in one single shot\footnote{To clarify, different from transfer learning, our single-shot setting refers to performing model pruning in only one forward (and sometimes backward) pass \cite{lee2018snip,SSEDS .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: To achieve single-shot pruning, a common practice is based on attribution scores \cite{taylor2017,ancona2020shapley .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In a nutshell, by quantifying the contribution from each individual model weight (i.e., embedding parameter in our case) to a performance metric, parameters with the lowest scores can be nullified to meet an arbitrary size constraint.
elements.sent-2411-036: In content-based recommendation, existing solutions \cite{SSEDS,wang2023single perform parameter attribution by measuring the loss value change after removing the parameter from the full embedding table, which can be approximated with first-order gradients \cite{lee2018snip .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: If we treat all embedding parameters as cooperative ``players'' in a game, then this is essentially asking the question: In a team formed by all players, what is each individual player's contribution to the whole team? However, this formulation hardly aligns with the pruning context, where a player (i.e., embedding parameter), if not pruned, will actually form a smaller team with only a subset of players.
elements.sent-2411-038: In a single-shot pruning setting, instead of retaining embedding parameters that are most important to the full parameter set, we should ultimately identify parameters that can be of high value to any parameter subsets.
elements.sent-2411-039: So, the right question to be asked is: What is the expected contribution of each individual player, when it joins any team formed by a subset of players? Due to the intertwined dependency among parameters, it is clear that this question does not share the same answer with the former one.
elements.sent-2411-040: To answer this question, we aim to investigate embedding pruning for CRSs via the lens of cooperative games, where we employ Shapley value \cite{roth1988shapley to perform embedding parameter attribution.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Shapley value is a well-defined method in the cooperative game theory for fairly allocating the total game revenue to players based on their individual contributions \cite{shap2017,sage2020,jethani2021fastshap .}
elements.sent-2411-042: It quantifies each player's contribution by considering all possible team combinations, resulting in the expected contribution of the player in any group.
elements.sent-2411-043: As such, compared with existing practices \cite{lee2018snip,SSEDS , Shapley value is a better fit for generating unbiased attribution scores of embedding parameters to facilitate fast recommendation model pruning.}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, some major challenges are yet to be resolved before we can enjoy the benefit of Shapley values.
elements.sent-2411-045: Firstly, the computational costs of Shapley value are prohibitively expensive.
elements.sent-2411-046: In our context, when a CRS only activates a subset of embedding parameters, each parameter's contribution in this subset can be measured by comparing the CRS's recommendation performance with and without it.
elements.sent-2411-047: Then, a parameter's Shapley value is its average contribution to all possible subsets of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-048: To calculate all the exact Shapley values, we need to enumerate over all $2^{nd}$ parameter subsets, where $n$ and $d$ are respectively the numbers of features and embedding dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-049: Considering that in CRSs $nd$ is intractable (e.g., reaching 100 million in our biggest dataset), and reliably measuring the recommendation performance requires a sufficiently large dataset, the number of resulted forward passes has rendered the straightforward calculation of Shapley values an infeasible option.
elements.sent-2411-050: Secondly, most pruning methods employ a simple zero-out approach to remove less important parameters and create a sparse embedding matrix.
elements.sent-2411-051: While this reduces the model size, it can result in significant information loss as recommenders rely heavily on dense feature representations for accurate predictions.
elements.sent-2411-052: This further deteriorates in CRSs due to their extensive use of dot product \cite{deepfm2017,fm2010 and element-wise multiplication \cite{dcnv2 between embeddings to model feature interactions.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: By setting parameters to zero, pruning can disrupt the complex relationships between features, leading to suboptimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-054: Motivated by these challenges, we propose Shapley Value-guided Embedding Reduction (Shaver), a novel embedding pruning method for compressing a CRS model to any target size in one shot.
elements.sent-2411-055: Shaver assigns each embedding parameter a Shapley value as its attribution score, such that a well-trained model can prune its embeddings to any parameter budget in a single shot.
elements.sent-2411-056: To address the high computational costs of obtaining exact Shapley values, through a series of theoretical analyses, we develop an unbiased approximation method to reliably estimate the Shapley values of all embedding parameters.
elements.sent-2411-057: Efficiency-wise, Shaver reduces the computational complexity from $O((nd)! \times \len{\mathcal{D )$ to the much lower $O(md \times \len{\mathcal{D )$, with $\mathcal{D}$ and $m$ respectively being the dataset and number of feature fields (e.g., user gender and movie genre).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Notably, $m\ll n$ in our case -- tens of feature fields versus millions of features (see Table \ref{tab:dataset-stat ).}
elements.sent-2411-059: To reduce the information loss caused by the default zero-padding on pruned parameters, we propose an alternative that searches for an optimal placeholder value per feature field, termed the codebook, which is then used for padding nullified embedding parameters during inference.
elements.sent-2411-060: In addition, the codebook values can be solved in a closed form, making it better at retaining the recommendation performance with a negligible computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-061: In summary, our main contributions are:.
elements.sent-2411-062: We revisit one important but largely neglected task of single-shot embedding pruning, which is more relevant in the real-world, on-device deployment of CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-063: We propose Shaver, a novel embedding compression method that reduces the time complexity of Shapley value estimation for CTR models and provides a more effective alternative to the widely used zero-out approach in model pruning with a field-aware codebook.
elements.sent-2411-064: We conduct extensive experiments on three datasets to compare Shaver with state-of-the-art embedding compression methods.
elements.sent-2411-065: The results show that even without an extra fine-tuning step, Shaver can achieve competitive performance.
elements.sent-2411-066: CTR prediction plays a vital role in the development of online advertisements.
elements.sent-2411-067: One of the first and most influential backbones for CTR prediction is Factorization Machine (FM) \cite{fm2010 , where the interaction between features are explicitly model upto 2nd order.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Recently, with the rapid development of deep neural networks (DNN), various deep models have also been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-069: NeuMF \cite{neumf2017 combines the introduced Generalized Matrix Factorization (GMF) with a DNN network branch.}
elements.sent-2411-070: DeepFM \cite{deepfm2017 is another prime example, which adds a DNN branch into the FM model.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Unlike DeepFM, which only attempts to model up to 2nd-order interaction, DCNv2 \cite{dcnv2 explicitly models the $l + 1$-th order interaction by $l$ crossnet layers.}
elements.sent-2411-072: DCNv2 also incorporates a DNN to model more complex interactions.
elements.sent-2411-073: To further reduce the computation cost of DCNv2, Wang et al. \cite{dcnv2 also propose integrating Mixture-of-Experts for modeling the cross-layers, creating DCN--Mix.}
elements.sent-2411-074: A diverse set of research endeavors has proposed various methods for learning light-weight embeddings \cite{zhang2023experimental, tran2024thorough, li_survey2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Compositional embedding methods decompose the original embedding table into multiple smaller meta-embedding tables and share parameters between features \cite{qr2019,ttrec2021,dhe2021,memcom2020,legcf2024,coleman2023unified,cel2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Quantization approaches are also applied in light-weight RSs \cite{alpt2023,xu2021agile,yang2020mixed,guan2019post , which focus on reducing the loss of model quality in lower precision representation.}
elements.sent-2411-077: However, these approaches generally suffer from limited compression ratios \cite{zhang2023experimental .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Another line of research focuses on finding the best parameter allocation through Neural Architecture Search (NAS), which usually involves reinforcement learning \cite{nis2019, yunke_continous2023,qu2024scalable , bi-level optimization \cite{autoemb2021,autodim2021,kong2022autosrh , or evolutionary search \cite{rule2021,optembed2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Consequently, NAS-based approaches generally have high computation costs.
elements.sent-2411-080: Pruning is another popular approach to reduce the amount of embedding parameters by finding a binary mask \cite{pep2021, SSEDS,wang2024dynamic,amtl2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Recently, many hybrid methods \cite{cerp2023,optembed2022 have also been proposed to combine the benefit of various archetypes to achieve the best trade-off between performance and parameter budget.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Our method falls within the pruning category.
elements.sent-2411-083: Unlike most of other pruning methods \cite{pep2021,amtl2021 , we eliminate the hassle of simultaneously optimizing both the pruning budget mask and model performance.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Compared to \cite{qu2024scalable,rule2021 , which also can adapt to arbitrary parameter budgets but focus on collaborative filtering, our method tailored towards CRSs.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Additionally, unlike SSEDS \cite{SSEDS , which mandates uniform dimensions across all features within the same field, our method offers greater flexibility by allowing varied budget allocations without such constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Shapley values have been widely adopted in the explainable AI community due to its high correlation with human intuition \cite{shap2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Because of its high computation cost, various researches have been proposed to tackle this challenge through random order sampling \cite{montecarloShapley,sage2020 , solving weighted least square problem \cite{shap2017,covert2020improving , being model specific \cite{shap2017,treeshap2020 , and recently amortization approaches \cite{jethani2021fastshap,covert2024stochastic .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Recently, the success of Shapley values in explainable AI has inspired its application in pruning deep models.
elements.sent-2411-089: To start with, Ancona et al. \cite{ancona2020shapley pointed out several desirable properties of Shapley values in pruning neurons:.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Null player: If the game (loss) doesn't depend on a player (parameter or group of parameters), its attribution is zero.
elements.sent-2411-091: Symmetry: If the game depends on two players equally, then the two players should receive the same attribution.
elements.sent-2411-092: Efficiency: Attributions sum up to the difference between the loss evaluated when all players are enabled and the loss evaluated when all players are removed.
elements.sent-2411-093: Later, NeuronShapley \cite{ghorbani2020neuron considered Shapley value estimation as a multi-arm bandit problem, and applied truncated Monte Carlo to estimate Shapley values.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Leveraging the multi-arm bandit settings, Guan et al. \cite{guan2022few employ an $\epsilon$-greedy approach.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Troupe \cite{troupe2021 proposes using Shapley values to select models for an ensemble based on a specific data point.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Last, although it is not applied for compressing embedding tables, Shapley values also have been applied in recommender system designs \cite{ben2018game,fang2023autoshape .}

elements.sent-2411-167: Specifically, we are interested in answering the following research questions (RQs):.
elements.sent-2411-168: Compared with non-single-shot baselines, how does Shaver perform when compressing CRS models?.
elements.sent-2411-169: Compared with other single-shot baselines, how does Shaver perform under different parameter budgets?.
elements.sent-2411-170: Most on-device CRS compression methods require model retraining/finetuning post pruning.
elements.sent-2411-171: Does Shaver also benefit from such a practice?.
elements.sent-2411-172: What is the impact of dataset size to the recommendation performance of the pruned models?.
elements.sent-2411-173: Does Shaver exhibit any patterns when pruning embedding parameters?.

elements.sent-2411-192: For this comparison, we test three sparsity rate settings with $t=\{50\%, 80\%, 95\%\}$ as all baselines under this category needs to be retrained for every setting.
elements.sent-2411-193: Across three datasets, Shaver achieves competitive results with other non-single-shot pruning methods that require costly training steps for each parameter budget.
elements.sent-2411-194: Shaver demonstrates a strong performance compared to other methods, especially with an 80\% sparsity rate.
elements.sent-2411-195: While in the low sparsity rate, the performance gap between methods is small as it is generally easier to reduce parameters.

elements.sent-2411-201: Notably, different from RQ1, as all baselines tested here are all single-shot methods, we have been able to test with a wider variety of sparsity rates $t=\{0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6, 0.8, 0.9, 0.95, 0.99, 0.999\}$.
elements.sent-2411-203: Shaver-Codebook is the most competitive method for KDD and Criteo, retaining the performance even with only 10\% and 5\% parameters of the original models, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-204: The second best is Shaver-Zero, whose performance is similar to Shaver-Codebook in the Avazu dataset.
elements.sent-2411-205: These results indicate promising potential in applying the Shapley value in compressing models for CTR problems.
elements.sent-2411-206: Taylor is the third best as they also utilize information from the training and validation datasets, especially in the Criteo dataset, where the difference is only noticeable under high-sparsity settings.
elements.sent-2411-207: PTQ provides strong performance initially but could not hold up at a higher sparsity rate, as it allocates the same memory budget for all features, limiting their performance.

elements.sent-2411-209: This experiment also mimics scenarios where a deployed recommendation model has sufficient computing resources to perform further on-device updates.
elements.sent-2411-210: While there is some variation in results across different datasets, in most cases, fine-tuning models further enhance their performance or maintain it at its current level, or with minimal loss.
elements.sent-2411-211: However, occasionally, fine-tuning can lead to decreased model performance, particularly for some settings in KDD.
elements.sent-2411-212: The reason for the performance drop is that Shaver uses the validation set to estimate Shapley values, which can cause data leakage and potentially degrade the final checkpoint performance post-fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-213: Moreover, fine-tuning seems to have a larger effect on high sparsity rates with Shaver-Zero, where performance degradation is more pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-214: Figure \ref{fig:tradeoff-dataset-size depicts the trade-off between the performance and the proportion $p$ of the full dataset $\mathcal{D}$ employed.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Note that $\mathcal{D}$ combines both training and validation samples in our setting.
elements.sent-2411-216: Under all configurations, we guarantee the algorithms have converged and seen all data.
elements.sent-2411-217: Empirically, the required runtime is linearly scaled with the dataset size employed, as Shaver generally converges after iterating through the whole dataset once.
elements.sent-2411-218: In the Criteo and KDD datasets, the amount of data employed has a negligible effect on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-219: Conversely, in the Avazu dataset, the volume of data has a markedly greater impact.
elements.sent-2411-220: One possible hypothesis for this discrepancy is that despite containing more features than the Criteo dataset, the Avazu dataset has fewer instances.
elements.sent-2411-221: Consequently, the validation dataset is insufficient to represent the actual data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-222: Nonetheless, as our methods only require forward passes, we can effortlessly parallelize the computation compared to other training-required methods.
elements.sent-2411-223: Moreover, the overhead remains minimal, even with the entire Criteo dataset -- our worst case as our computation cost increases with the number of fields -- Shaver takes approximately 2.5 hours to compute the Shapley value.
elements.sent-2411-224: In comparison, for any given parameter budget, training each method generally takes over 1 hour, without including the time needed for hyperparameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-225: We aim to unveil the key difference between the traditional zero-out approach and our codebook-based approach in compressed models.
elements.sent-2411-226: Therefore, employing DCN-Mix with a 50\% sparsity rate, we split each dataset's feature into three different bins based on frequency so that each bin had the same number of features.
elements.sent-2411-227: Figure \ref{fig:rq4 shows the average embedding size assigned per feature in each group.}
elements.sent-2411-228: The codebook approach assigns less parameter budget to high frequency.
elements.sent-2411-229: One plausible explanation is that the codebook already contained parameters representing the high-frequency features.
elements.sent-2411-230: Thus, the model could have a larger budget to allocate to the lower frequency features, improving the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-231: In this paper, we propose Shaver, which employs Shapley value to fairly distribute each embedding parameter contribution and effectively prune the embedding table in CTR models.
elements.sent-2411-232: Shaver first provide an efficient method to calculate the Shapley value, and then implement a novel field-aware codebook quantization to represent the removed parameters.
elements.sent-2411-233: Extensive experiments show that Shaver achieves competitive performance without expensive retraining and fine-tuning steps for each parameter budget.

elements.sent-2411-255: In this section, we provide more details on our experiment settings.
elements.sent-2411-256: We compare Shaver with the following embedding pruning methods for on-device content-based recommendation:.
elements.sent-2411-257: QR \cite{qr2019 divides the original embedding table into two smaller embedding tables (commonly called meta-embedding tables).}
elements.sent-2411-258: The meta-embedding vectors are aggregrated by multiplication to create the final embedding vector.
elements.sent-2411-259: TTRec \cite{ttrec2021 tackles the challenge of excessive embedding table parameters with a sequence of matrix products, employing tensor-train decomposition.}
elements.sent-2411-260: PEP \cite{pep2021 adopts a Soft-Thresholding Reparametrization (STR) trick to iteratively remove redundant parameters in the first training step.}
elements.sent-2411-261: OptEmbed \cite{optembed2022 involves three consecutive steps: Train supernet and remove excessive features, evolutionary search for optimal embedding size for each field, and finally, retrain the network from the found embedding mask.}
elements.sent-2411-262: CERP \cite{cerp2023 incorporate STR into two equal-size embedding tables $\mathbf{E _1$ and $\mathbf{E _2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: To compensate for the two sparse meta-embedding tables, the authors integrate a regularization loss into the training loss and combine two meta-embedding vectors with a sum operation, resulting in a dense vector.
elements.sent-2411-264: MagPrune \cite{tran2024thorough prune the trained embedding table by their magnitude.}
elements.sent-2411-265: In line with our method, MagPrune also compresses the model by pruning the trained model.
elements.sent-2411-266: Taylor \cite{taylor2017 proposes to prune the model with the first-order Taylor approximation of the loss function.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Similar to our method, this method also employs feedback from the dataset to calculate the attribution scores.
elements.sent-2411-268: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) \cite{ptq2016,ptq2021 first linearly scales the weight into $[-2^{b - 1 , 2^{b - 1 - 1]$ range, where $b$ is the bit width used.}}}
elements.sent-2411-269: Then we round up the result, converting parameters from float to int.
elements.sent-2411-270: When performing inference, these compressed parameters would be scaled back to float32 with the stored bias and scale.
elements.sent-2411-271: For Taylor and our methods, we pruned the original checkpoints as specified.
elements.sent-2411-272: For PTQ, we compressed the models from float32 to (4, 8, 16)-bit integers, the sparsity rate is calculated as $1 - b / 32$.
elements.sent-2411-273: During training, Shaver's codebook $\mathbf{C}$ is freezed.

elements.sent-2411-274: The dataset preprocess steps are described below:.
elements.sent-2411-275: Criteo \cite{Criteo contains ad click data over a week.}
elements.sent-2411-276: Following the winning solution from the original Criteo Challenge, we discretize each value $x$ in numeric feature fields to $\lceil {\text{log _2(x) \rceil$ if $x > 2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-277: We replace infrequent features, which appear less than min\_count = $10$ times, with out-of-vocabulary (OOV) tokens for each field.
elements.sent-2411-278: Avazu \cite{Avazu includes 10 days of click-through data.}
elements.sent-2411-279: As a common practice, we first remove the `id' field, which has a unique value for every record.
elements.sent-2411-280: Similar to Criteo dataset, we remove infrequent features and replace them with OOV tokens (min\_count = 2).
elements.sent-2411-281: KDD \cite{KDD consists of records gathered from search session logs.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Similar to previous two datasets, we utilize OOV tokens to represent infrequent features (min\_count = 10).
elements.sent-2411-283: For Criteo and Avazu dataset, we directly adopt the split and preprocessed data from \cite{tran2024thorough .}

elements.sent-2411-285: For DeepFM backbone, our methods achieves the best results in all settings.
elements.sent-2411-286: With the DeepFM backbone, where the original model has poor performance, our method can remove excessive parameters and improve model performance with fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-287: Thus, model fine-tuning has a more positive effect on the DeepFM backbone.
elements.sent-2411-288: Except for the Avazu dataset, codebook approaches outperform zero baseline slightly on Criteo, and significantly on KDD.
elements.sent-2411-289: The reason for this exception is unclear.
elements.sent-2411-290: One plausible explanation is that zero padding can act as a regularization and better prevent overfitting compared to codebook approaches.
elements.sent-2411-291: Regarding single-shot comparison, in general, the performance trend is similar to DCN, with few exceptions.
elements.sent-2411-292: First, PTQ achieves worse relative results in the Criteo dataset.
elements.sent-2411-293: Second, as mentioned above, Shaver-Zero outperforms Shaver-Codebook and other methods in Avazu settings.
